coredata.dc:description: Energy experts predict that the world will consume energy at a rate of 30TW by 2050 to maintain economic growth. This large need emphasizes the importance of using renewable energy resources. Among the different renewable energy resources, solar energy is significant because of its universal availability and free high-energy power. Researchers have developed third-generation solar cells in response to the high manufacturing and installation costs of first-generation solar cells and the poor performance of second-generation solar cells. The current focus is on devices that are economical and highly efficient. This new generation of solar cells is made from organic materials (polymers), nanotubes, silicon wires, organic dyes, conductive plastics, etc., and there is plenty of room to enhance performance and reduce costs. Recent studies demonstrate the progressive increase in solar cell research on polymer and plastic devices using hybrid approaches by introducing nanomaterials into polymer-based and thin-film photovoltaic devices. The success and employment of these novel nanotechnology concepts could provide cheap, lightweight, and flexible solar cells that are highly efficient. In this study, the use of nanomaterials in different parts of nanostructured solar cells has been investigated to understand the effects of their size, morphology, etc.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of nanostructured solar cell examples on unit cell scale: from an all-organic cell to an all-inorganic solid-state cell [6]. 3D, three-dimensional; DSH, dye-sensitized heterojunction; DSSC, dye-sensitized solar cell; ETA, extremely thin absorber.

(A) The structure of a bulk heterojunction solar cell and (B) an ordered heterojunction solar cell. (1) The excitons created by incident light are diffusing toward a D–A interface; (2) the hole travelling toward the electrode; (3) the electron moving toward the electrode; (4) the hole trapped in an isolated island of organic molecule (in bulk heterojunction). The charge carriers that are trapped within the randomly separated materials and unable to reach the electrodes are responsible for the lower performance. In the OHJ design in which the donor–acceptor material positions were controlled via the cell design, the charge carriers were not trapped within these orderly separated materials [20,22]. ITO, indium tin oxide.
Classification of BCPs on the basis of their structure and their role in BHJSCs [23]. BCP, block copolymer; BHJSC, bulk heterojunction solar cell.
Main types of BCPs used for PV applications: (A) coil–coil, (B) rod–coil, and (C) rod–rod diblock copolymers [16]. BCP, block copolymer; PV, photovoltaic.

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 8
chapter.ce:title: Novel Nanomaterials for Solar Cell Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National University of Malaysia (UKM), Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia
University of Applied Science & Technology, Mashhad, Khorasan Razavi, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Energy experts predict that the world will consume energy at a rate of 30TW by 2050 to maintain economic growth. This large need emphasizes the importance of using renewable energy resources. Among the different renewable energy resources, solar energy is significant because of its universal availability and free high-energy power. Researchers have developed third-generation solar cells in response to the high manufacturing and installation costs of first-generation solar cells and the poor performance of second-generation solar cells. The current focus is on devices that are economical and highly efficient. This new generation of solar cells is made from organic materials (polymers), nanotubes, silicon wires, organic dyes, conductive plastics, etc., and there is plenty of room to enhance performance and reduce costs. Recent studies demonstrate the progressive increase in solar cell research on polymer and plastic devices using hybrid approaches by introducing nanomaterials into polymer-based and thin-film photovoltaic devices. The success and employment of these novel nanotechnology concepts could provide cheap, lightweight, and flexible solar cells that are highly efficient. In this study, the use of nanomaterials in different parts of nanostructured solar cells has been investigated to understand the effects of their size, morphology, etc.

ce:section.ce:para: A solar cell is a device that converts solar energy, a clean and vital renewable energy source, into electricity and can help to overcome the global energy crisis. Although commercial solar cells exhibit good performance and durability, there are still many ways to improve the performance and cost of solar cells through collaborations among scientists, technicians, and industrial workers. Among different technologies, nanotechnology has shown highly promising contributions to common techniques by improving their performance via changing their fundamental properties. More research on tailored nanostructures for solar cells will increase the potential for developing next-generation solar cells with enhanced performance and lower costs. To reach this goal, it is vital to use nanostructured materials in solar cells. The distinct physical properties of nanomaterials, such as their flexible bandgap, enhanced optical path, and considerably decreased charge carrier recombination, depend on their size and shape. These properties can improve light trapping and photo collection via novel approaches, making nanostructured materials attractive and flexible materials in solar cells. Nanostructured materials could not only improve conventional solar cells via different approaches but also be used to create cost-effective materials with high conversion efficiencies by employing low-cost production methods [1]. Because third-generation solar cells often use nanomaterials with different characteristics, this chapter focuses on third-generation solar cells to demonstrate novel results obtained by employing nanomaterials in different types of nanostructured solar cells. Different classifications are available for nanostructured solar cells from different scholars, including dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), quantum dot–sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs), hybrid solar cells, perovskite solar cells (PSCs), and organic solar cells [2–4], which leads to many overlaps among the different classifications. Fig. 8.1 shows the classifications according to the amounts of inorganic and organic materials used in the solar cells [5,6]. In this chapter, this classification is considered for differentiating the types of solar cells, although in some sections, overlaps can be seen.

The bulk heterojunction solar cell (BHJSC) consists of 100% organic nanomaterials. The electron–conductor/hole–conductor mixture contains a polymer–polymer blend or a polymer–organic molecule blend. Hybrid solar cells contain less organic material. The structure of a hybrid cell is based on a nanoporous network of n-type oxide particles, such as TiO2, covered with a thin, light-sensitive shell, and the entire porous structure is immersed in a p-type hole conductor of various polymer types. In hybrid solar cells, named DSSCs or Gratzel cells, the light-sensitive shell or absorber is a monomolecular layer of organic dye, and the hole conductor is a liquid electrolyte.

By replacing the liquid electrolyte with a p-type solid semiconductor material, such as CuSCN, CuBr, or Co or Cu derivatives [7–9], or organic molecules, e.g., poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT), ruthenium complexes, etc. [10–12], the solar cells are then categorized as DSH or solid-state DSSCs. In a solid-state solar cell, when the organic dye is replaced with a solid-state absorber, such as CdTe, a-Si, or CuInS2, the cells are called extremely thin absorber (ETA) cells. In three-dimensional (3D) solar cells, the absorber plays a role in both absorption and hole conduction [13].

The photovoltaic effect was first observed in a single crystal of anthracene sandwiched between two identical electrodes and illuminated from one side by Kallmann and Pope [14], and the first improvement to its performance involved the introduction of the bilayer heterojunction concept. To overcome the disadvantages of this concept, the bulk heterojunction architecture concept was introduced, bringing the interface to the exciton instead of bringing the exciton to the interface. BHJSCs, which are based on conjugated polymers or small molecules, are considered one of the most promising solar cell types because of their compatibility with roll-to-roll printing techniques, low cost, low weight, and versatile organic material properties, which include their design, light weight, mechanical flexibility, abundant sources of raw materials, and flexibility. The mixture of regioregular P3HT and its derivatives with fullerene or its soluble derivatives are often employed in BHJSCs [15,16]. The development of new donor polymers and the improvement of the device architecture and fabrication are among the factors that remarkably increased the performance of BHJSCs. Massive progress has been made to increase their efficiencies by developing improved materials and device structures, and the power conversion efficiency has exceeded 11% [17]. However, to make polymer solar cells competitive with inorganic solar cells, more improvements to their stability and performance are needed [16]. For the further development of BHJSCs, it is vital to control, understand, and manipulate the morphology and nanostructure of the active layer because it is the component with the most influence on solar cell performance [18].

In BHJSCs, donor (D) and acceptor (A) materials are interspersed to decrease the average distance between the donor and acceptor molecules without reducing the overall thickness of the active layers of the device [19,20]. Because the main issues affecting the performance of conjugated polymer solar cells (BHJSCs) are their nanomorphology, charge carrier mobility and spectral sensitivity, the modification and improvement of these factors will likely enhance their performance. Different methods have been developed by researchers to successfully improve BHJSC performance, such as using novel materials, including double-cable polymers, regioregular polymers, and low-bandgap polymers, and design control. Other disadvantages of BHJSCs, such as low adhesion of the thin film onto the polymer substrate and the photodegradation and chemical degradation in some polymer composites, must also be considered. These issues strongly depend on surface bonding and the quality of the adsorption sites for the molecules involved, which can be addressed by choosing the proper substrate and etching and bonding materials [20]. To increase the quantum efficiency in the highly disordered bulk heterojunction, an attempt has been made to create an ordered heterojunction (OHJ) by controlling the position of the donor–acceptor (see Fig. 8.2 ) [21]. For further performance improvements of OHJs, it is vital to enhance the carrier mobility by controlling the morphology of the pores using suitable processing parameters.

Chen et al. [23] reviewed conjugated nanostructured-polymer preparation methods, such as layer-by-layer deposition, template methods, nanoimprint lithography, emulsion polymerization, and electrophoretic deposition (EPD), for organic solar cell applications. The photophysical properties of conjugated polymers were directly influenced by their nanoscale geometries, such as nanoparticles, aggregates, nanofibers (NFs), or thin films. To optimize their performance, different parameters, such as the material synthesis methods, morphology control, structure design, and device fabrication must be investigated in depth [23,24].

Block copolymers (BCPs) are among the most widely used materials in BHJSCs because the nanostructure of these semiconducting polymers is easily controlled, which is a critical parameter for developing BHJSCs [16].

Yassar et al. [16] classified BCPs, according to their chemical structures, as coil or rod. Fig. 8.3 shows the classification of block polymers based on their structures and roles. Rod BCPs, which have a higher rigidity, consist of conjugated polymers with interactions between the π-conjugated and aromatic systems, and coil blocks with higher flexibility were made from the aliphatic main chains. There are various parameters that affect the morphology of prepared nanostructures, such as the relative volume fraction of one block with respect to the other, the reciprocal miscibility, and the lengths of the block segments. Notably, the solvent could also affect the final morphology of prepared blocks [25–27].

Different types of BCPs have been used for solar cells (see Fig. 8.4 ), and they are listed as follows: coil–coil BCPs (both the donor and acceptor are attached as side groups to the main chain, which is not conjugated); rod–coil BCPs (one block is formed by a π-conjugated system as the main chain and the second one is a coil block with acceptors attached as side groups to the main chain); and rod–rod BCPs (copolymers with the main chain made of two blocks with different π-conjugated systems). It is important to mention that there are more complex systems such as triblock copolymers [28,29], cross-linked copolymers [30–32], or BCPs with blocks made from a mixture of different co-monomers.

Notably, rod–coil and all-conjugated BCPs have the ability to self-assemble into well-ordered morphologies, and the structures usually play an important role in determining the physical properties of the materials. This ability could provide the opportunity to wisely design and control these materials at the nanoscale to optimize their performance in organic photovoltaic devices [33,34]. Fig. 8.5A
shows the equilibrium morphologies formed by microphase separation of coil–coil diblock copolymers (DBCs) in the bulk. It must be remembered that switching one or both coil blocks with a conjugated block that adopts a semiflexible or rigid rod structure could also qualitatively change this phase diagram [35]. Fig. 8.5B presents different types of hierarchical microstructures [34,36,37].
Fig. 8.6 shows some samples of donor–acceptor (D–A), rod–coil, and coil–coil block copolymers that are used as active layers in bulk heterojunction solar cells [16,38,39].

Some researchers have used the donor–acceptor rod–coil BCP as compatibilizers in BHJSCs [16]. Most surfactants such as amphiphilic diblock copolymer additives, which can control morphology at the nanoscale [40], have been used as compatibilizers in BHJSCs [41–43] (see Fig. 8.7 ). The proper compatibilizer could control the size of the donor and acceptor domains and improve the phase stability in BHJSCs by reducing the interfacial energy and limiting the coalescence among immiscible components in condensed phases.

The rod–coil and coil–coil BCPs with one or two sacrificial blocks are used in BHJSCs [16,44], but this subject needs further exploration. Researchers have used many different types of polymers and BCPs in BHJSCs [16,45–48]. Although promising progress in the synthesis of D-A BCPs has been made, finding the proper synthesis method, controlling self-assembly, and increasing the performance of D-A BCPs are still challenges requiring more attention. Determining the appropriate chemical design of the BCP and optimizing the device fabrication require interdisciplinary research in the following areas: material synthesis with carefully chosen comonomers or precursor materials; morphology control considering the solvent and solvent-additives roles; optimization of the interfacial materials to enhance the charge extraction rate and to lower the energy barrier; adoption of different electron acceptors for improved energy harvesting to match the energy level; device design engineering, such as inverted and tandem structures, to increase the device performance; and use of a triple-junction tandem cell instead of the double-junction cell for improved performance [17]. Fullerene and its derivatives are well-known acceptors in BHJ-OSC. Ganesamoorthy et al. [47] extensively reviewed the recent developments of fullerene derivatives (see Fig. 8.8 ). Asymmetrical PC71BM with high optical absorption in the visible region is considered a primary candidate for solar cell applications. Fullerene multiadducts have shown better performances compared with PC61/71BM. Meanwhile, multifunctionalization showed better results. C60- and C70-based hetero bis-adducts, such as C60(CH2) (Ind), and OQMF70, exhibit high performances, whereas indene-substituted C60 and C70 fullerene-based bis-adducts showed the highest efficiency. Despite the considerable progress in this field, according to Ganesamoorthy et al. [47], it is important to investigate methods to carefully tailor the new fullerenes to exhibit properties such as good solubility, higher electron mobility, and good miscibility with the donor material. Meanwhile, device engineering, process optimizing, etc., also need to be explored for further development.

Graphene as an exclusive two-dimensional (2D) nanostructure with unique mechanical, optical, and electronic properties is considered one of the most attractive materials for use in different types of solar cells. Graphene, graphene derivatives, e.g., graphene oxide (GO) and reduced GO, graphene hybrids, and its functionalized forms have been used as the photoanode, counter electrode (CE), acceptor, and transporting layers in solar cells [49,50]. Functionalization is a very important strategy for developing new hybrid materials with novel and modified properties. The properties of various carbon-based nanomaterials, such as fullerene, carbon nanodots, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and graphene, could improve with the addition of molecular moieties that possess the desired properties for a specified application. According to investigations by Zhang et al. [51], through the proper alignment of the energy levels of the different components or moieties, it is possible to control charge generation, transfer and recombination, and energy output, which is a very important parameter for various devices including organic solar cells. The type of dopant materials and carbon-based nanomaterials and the chemical nature of the linker could determine the final efficiency of device (see Fig. 8.9 ).

Some researchers [52,53] have reviewed the various applications of graphene in different types of polymer solar cells, including the preparation methods, doping, surface treatment and functionalization of graphene [54]. The application of graphene as an acceptor and active layer in polymer solar cells was investigated [55,56]. Photoanodes and CEs based on graphene have been studied by some researchers [57–62]. Dai et al. [54] reviewed edge-functionalized graphene as a hole-transporting material (HTM) in the active layer, edge-functionalized GO as an electron-extraction layer, basal-plane-functionalized GO as a hole-extraction layer, and basal-plane-functionalized graphene as an electron-transport material in the active layer of polymer solar cells. For covalent attachments of various chemical materials to graphene, the dangling bonds at the edge of graphene are the proper position while the graphene basal plane could be modified via either covalent or noncovalent functionalization. To self-assemble graphene sheets into hierarchically structured materials, asymmetric functionalization must be applied in the two opposite surfaces of the individual graphene sheets with different moieties (see Fig. 8.10 ) [54].

A fiber-shaped polymer solar cell was developed by Liu et al. [61] as CE using a polymer-coated steel wire (primary electrode) wrapped with a single-layer graphene sheet (Fig. 8.11A ). The employed poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) sheet used to transfer the graphene could be either retained on the fiber or washed away by acetone. Fig. 8.11B shows the preparation methods for a fiber solar cell consisting of spin-coated PMMA on the Cu-supported graphene layer, etching of the Cu substrate, Au doping of graphene, and wrapping the PMMA (with graphene) onto the polymer layers to make the device. The device presented a moderately high efficiency among the reported fiber solar cells which used solid-state polymers as the active layer.

Figs. 8.12 and 8.13
present some commonly used polymer structures as donors and acceptors in a polymer solar cell application.

Solution-processed small molecules are another promising substitute for conjugated polymers for BHJSCs owing to their easy purification, well-defined and versatile molecular structure, definite molecular weight, lower batch-to-batch variation, and ease toward large-scale production. The studies revealed that through the careful design of the small molecules, their absorption characteristics, energy levels, charge carrier mobility, and even photoelectric properties could be controlled [63–68]. The chemical structures of small molecules that were used as electron donors with high efficiencies in BHJSCs are depicted in Fig. 8.14 .

Gautam et al. [65] investigated the influence of nanoscale morphology and charge transport in small molecules on the performance of BHJSCs. They revealed that improved nanoscale morphology and more balanced charge transport from the solvent additive could increase the efficiency of the solar cell (see Fig. 8.15 ).

Inorganic–organic hybrid solar cells (hybrid solar cells) have attracted considerable interest as a result of the synergistic properties of organic and inorganic semiconductors, which also take advantage of lightweight, robust, flexible, and inexpensive properties. Inorganic semiconductor nanomaterials (SNMs) (on the nanoscale in at least one of their dimensions) are dispersed in an organic polymer matrix.

The nanomaterials used in hybrid solar cells have a lower optical absorption compared with conducting polymers and can absorb a much larger portion of the solar spectrum. Meanwhile, the nanomaterials could liberate and transport the potential charge carriers that are created through the absorption by the polymer host. In photovoltaic processes, the interfacial charge separation is considered critical to the function of the device, and having large interfaces could lead to greater opportunities for the excitons to reach the interfaces, which would result in a higher conversion efficiency. Nanostructured composites are the most studied hybrid solar cells. Liu [69] studied different combinations of nanostructures with polymers to determine the critical parameters that influence the photoinduced charge separation–transportation processes to improve the overall power conversion efficiency. Fig. 8.16 presents a schematic of different hybrid solar cell designs.

Choosing the proper electronic structure for inorganic semiconductor materials for hybrid solar cells is very important for improving their performance (see Fig. 8.17 ). The main materials used contain cadmium compounds, silicon, metal oxide nanoparticles, and low-bandgap nanoparticles. Metal halide PSCs, which consist of the perovskite structure AMX3 (ACH3NH3, NH2CHNH2, Cs; MPb, Sn, Ge; XI, Br, Cl, etc.), are light-absorbing materials, and among different kinds of hybrid solar cells, they have shown one of the best performances by reaching a conversion efficiency of 22.1% [70].

Liu [69] discovered some important factors that affect hybrid solar cell performance. The current density, which influences the performance of solar cells, could be controlled with the charge transportation along the inorganic and organic components. Nanorods (NRs) and nanotube (NT) hybrid solar cells demonstrated better performance because their continuous network greatly reduces the need for charge carriers to hop between nanoparticles during transportation to the electrode [73]. The charge separation efficiency was directly affected by the interfacial area and the contact between the inorganic and organic components. Therefore, NT array-based systems with higher surface areas perform better compared with NR array-based systems with similar sizes. For the development of hybrid solar cells, it is vital to choose proper components by considering their energy-level alignment at the interfaces. It is important to use nanostructures with large interfaces, such as long NRs/NTs, branched nanopods, or porous networks, while considering the structural design for better charge transportation through the connection of each component. In consideration of this issue, ordered NR/NT arrays are good candidates. Another requirement to improve the charge separation at the contact interface is the establishment of good contact between the organic and inorganic components in a hybrid solar cell. To achieve this objective, Rath et al. used in situ synthesis of mixed components followed by annealing [74] to find the optimum condition for blending. Meanwhile, using alternate capping groups for the inorganic nanoparticles and incorporating small organic dye molecules are other options. Because the mobility of holes at conducting polymers is lower compared with the mobility of electrons in inorganic semiconductors, improvements to the nanostructured network of a conducting polymer in the hybrid system are needed. Another option to improve the cell efficiency is to use the structural design, which could break or limit the symmetry of the inorganic–organic blended layer because this symmetry provides the same chances for charge carrier movement to both electrodes, thus reducing the effective power conversion [75].
According to research from Jeltsch et al. [76], NR and QD composites form superior interconnected pathways the movement of electrons in hybrid solar cells.

Various methods for the preparation and control of the size and shape of nanoscale inorganic semiconductors in hybrid solar cells have been investigated (Fig. 8.18 ) [74,77,78]. Because electrospinning is an economical, large-area, facile technique, it is developing rapidly for the fabrication of one-dimensional (1D) NFs, such as TiO2, ZnO, Cu2ZnSnS4 (CZTS), etc., in solar cells [77, 79–81]. It is important to mention that for all preparation methods in hybrid solar cells, the discovery of new material combinations and new precursors in combination with the optimization morphology and rational design of hybrid interfaces is essential for the further improvement of hybrid solar cells [74].

Sundarrajan et al. [82] succeeded in fabricating P3HT and P3HT/PCBM NF cloths by co-electrospinning P3HT or P3HT/PCBM as the core material and PVP as the shell material for PV applications. BCPs also showed promising results as polymer components in hybrid solar cells because of their unique properties of self-assembly, facile synthesis, etc. [83–85].

Hu et al. [86] showed that various carbon materials play a great role in the development of PSCs by improving charge transfer. The modification of carbon materials by combining them or doping, etc., could improve their performance and stability [87–89].

ETA solar cells contain an ultra-thin light-absorbing semiconducting layer deposited on a large branched or 1D surface of an inorganic semiconductor, followed by an appropriate wide-bandgap p-type semiconducting thin-film as the hole conductor. Fig. 8.19 presents the schematic of a basic ETA solar cell [90].

Different absorbers have been employed in ETA solar cells, such as CdSe, Sb2S3, In2S3, CdTe, CdS, PbS, ZnO, Cu2S, AgSbS2 and their alloys [92–102]. The formation of a homogenous coating to minimize recombination losses and prevent the blockage of nanostructure pores is very important for ETA solar cells. Therefore, determining an appropriate deposition method plays a very important role. Electrodeposition [96–99,101], aerosol-assisted chemical vapor deposition [91], chemical bath deposition (CBD) [103,104], sputtering [100], screen printing [92], and solvothermal synthesis [102,105] are some of the methods employed for the preparation of ETA solar cells. Finding the proper deposition method to prepare a uniform coating is a challenge in ETA solar cells, and it is necessary to consider the type and properties of the materials.

In a typical DSSC, dye-sensitized photoanodes often consist of a mesoporous wide-bandgap semiconductor (WBGS) layer such as TiO2 covered by a monolayer of charge transfer dye, which is attached to the conducting glass (working electrode) and a CE separated by a salt electrolyte [106]. The development of DSSC versus QDSSC is determined by the type of sensitizer used, which is either a dye (DSSCs) or inorganic nanoparticles (QDs) [107]. By considering and using the unique optoelectronic characteristics of QDs, such as the size-dependent tunable energy bandgap and carrier multiplication (i.e., multiple exciton generation effect [MEG]) [108–110], the performance of QDSSCs can be improved [111]. Cd chalcogenides, such as CdS and CdSe, have been widely studied as a semiconductor material for QDSSCs [112]. Different configurations of QD-based solar cells, such as photoelectrodes comprising QD assemblies, QDSSCs, and QDs dispersed in organic semiconductor polymer matrices [113], are depicted in Fig. 8.20 . QDSSCs, which contain QD sensitizers, are promising candidates to replace DSSCs considering their significant improvement in the energy conversion efficiency (ECE) (e.g., from 1% to beyond 11%) in recent years [3,114]. QDSSCs are classified as (1) photoelectrodes comprising QD assemblies, (2) QDSSCs, and (3) QDs dispersed in organic semiconductor polymer matrices [113], as shown in Fig. 8.20.

Fig. 8.21 presents a schematic of the electron-transfer mechanism and photocurrent generation model for WBGSs, QDs, and solid and liquid electrolytes. Different approaches are available to improve QDSSC performance, such as bandgap engineering (the type of QD sensitizer and the size) and surface morphology engineering (type and surface morphology of the working electrode); additional steps can be taken to engineer materials to influence the MEG effect, to improve the role of electrolytes–redox mediator, and to understand the influence of different CE materials on solar cell performance [112,116]. Rauf et al. [115] employed an exclusive approach based on inferential statistics to review QD-based solar cells data. They determined that the sensitizer plays the most important role in determining the final efficiency in both liquid and all solid-state solar cells. The electrolyte type shows no effect on the efficiency, but it plays a dual role, supporting and opposing the fill factor. They claimed that the photoelectrode and its preparation method have 72% contribution to the efficiency, while the combination of the photoelectrode with electrolyte has 90% contribution to the fill factor. Then, they propose more research focused on the development of the photoelectrode and the electrolyte for improving the efficiency and fill factor in the solar cells.

Kumar et al. [3] studied the recent developments in QD sensitizers including single-core QDs [112,117,118], core–shell structures [119–121], alloy structures [122,123], QDs with dopants [124,125], and green QDs [126,127]. Other structures, such as hybrids of nanoparticles with organic molecules (e.g., fullerenes, molecular imprinted polymers, and CNTs), inorganic molecules (e.g., silica, inorganic NTs, nanospheres, and QDs), or a mixture of organic and inorganic molecules (e.g., CNTs functionalized with ferrocene and Au nanoparticles modified with calixarenes) [50,128,129], have been extensively reviewed by Kumar et al. [3,130–133]. The development of new photoelectrodes, CE, sensitizers, and electrolytes using novel materials with different morphologies and employing cutting-edge techniques show promise of increased efficiency in QDSSCs. Notably, these developments could also be applied to DSSCs.

Although different methods are available for the preparation of QDs and their coupling to semiconductor materials, they are commonly divided into the following two main categories: in situ preparation and attachment of presynthesized colloidal quantum dot (CQD) (see Fig. 8.22 ). In situ preparation methods such as CBD and successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) have been widely used because of their simple procedures and low costs. However, the other deposition methods, such as direct adsorption, spray pyrolysis [134], chemical vapor deposition (CVD), [135], etc., have been used for QDSSCs. Because each deposition method has pros and cons, selection of the proper method depends on the objective of the specified application. For strong electronic coupling between the QD layer and the semiconductor, in situ preparation methods such as CBD or SILAR are recommended. However, for more control over the size and shape of the QD, ex situ methods such as EPD or molecule linker attachment [112] are preferred. Notably, the use of a linker to reduce the size distribution of QDs could result in a lower efficiency. One possible approach toward increasing QD loading on the photoanode during direct deposition methods is through the use of microwave irradiation, which should be further investigated [111].

Recent research in QDSSCs has revealed that the main issues directly affecting their performance are light trapping, interface between QDs and photoelectrodes, interfacial charge transfer, and device modeling. The photoanode is an important part of QDSSCs because its specific surface area indicates the area available for QD adsorption and its surface acts as a medium for collecting and transporting electrons from the QDs to the external circuit. These characteristics affect the photocurrent response and net photo conversion efficiency, which influence the QDSSCs performance. Sharma et al. [111] extensively reviewed various aspects of photoanodes to improve solar cell performance, such as the effects of the structure, morphology, and surface treatment, and the use of carbon-related materials, doped metal oxides, alternative photoanodic materials, blocking layers, or novel sensitizers. However, the development of new morphologic structures for higher QD absorption and rapid electron transportation is very important for QDSSC efficiency. Meanwhile, a proper bandgap arrangement among the conducting substrate, WBGS, QDs, and the compact layer between the FTO and metal oxide is important for increasing solar cell performance.

Considering the above-mentioned issues, it is important to continue to investigate 1D nanostructures with a large surface area, such as NTs, nanowires (NWs), and NRs, to improve the attachment of QDs and the transport of electrons and to lower the recombination rate of charge carriers, which oxidize the QDs on the surface of the oxide and within the electrolyte compartment. The adsorption of noble metals to the photoanode materials is another helpful option in controlling the rate of charge carrier transportation at the interface; the introduction of carbon nanostructures such as CNTs and graphene on the photoanodes could improve photogenerated electron transportation. Another important parameter that prevents electrons from transferring to the redox couple, which decreases the efficiency of solar cells, is QD surface passivation. Because graphene-semiconductor hybrid nanomaterials, CNTs, and metal nanomaterials (MNMs) could improve the charge carrier transportation with a significant reduction in recombination, they are considered favorable materials for decreasing the manufacturing costs of solar cells. Meanwhile, other 2D materials, such as transition metal chalcogenides (TMDs) and phosphorene heterojunctions, are considered promising materials for increasing solar cell performance [3]. Another proposed method to increase QD adsorption on the photoanode is to increase the surface roughness of WBGSs via etching [111,136].

TiO2, ZnO, and SnO2 are the most commonly used wide bandgap materials for photoanodes in QDSSC, although some researchers have reported using different materials, such as Zn2SnO4, ZrO2, Nb3O7F, BaTiO3, and SrTiO3 [137–141].

Doping of the photoanodes using elements in groups III, IV, V, and VI is another promising method for improved efficiency [142–145]. To optimize the effect of doping at the photoanode or QDs (or both), deeper understanding of optoelectrical characteristics of the WBGS/QD system is essential. Providing defects or hydroxyl groups on the surface of photoanode to increase the dye attachment is another mentioned option to improve the DSSCs performance [146–149]. Modifying the bandgap of the photoanode semiconductor materials, such as TiO2 and ZnO, and using suitable cationic and anionic dopants have been reported to positively affect the efficiency of solar cells [150–153].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of Morphology on Electrode Performance in QDSSCs and DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that for increasing the solar cell performance, light harvesting must be improved. The use of sensitizers with a broad light absorption range and good charge separation could improve light harvesting. To achieve the optimal light harvesting, different factors such as using a high surface area semiconductor, which adsorbs more sensitizers, establishing effective contact between the oxidized sensitizers and the electrolyte for faster regeneration, using materials with more efficient reflection and light-scattering properties, and increasing the rate of electron transport from the sensitizer to the electron collecting layer must be considered. Because the working concept for DSSCs and QDSSCs are same, the presented data could apply for both types of SCs.

Recently, many methods have been used to produce various photoanode structures that optimize the QD-loading and light-scattering effects, such as 1D structures, e.g., NWs [154], NRs [155], NFs [156], NTs [157], hexagonal nanoplates (2D) [158], etc., and 3D structures, e.g., hierarchical structures. Hierarchical nanostructures consist of a typical tree with NW, NR, or NT as stem and multiple short NR branches, which provide the high surface area for QD loading and enhance electron transport rate (see Fig. 8.23 ) [111].

According to Sengupta et al. [159], different nanomorphologies show various patterns for electron flow and light scattering, which affect the performance of DSSCs (see Fig. 8.24 ). Whereas a 1D nanostructure increases the dye adsorption and direct electron transport, hierarchical structures result in the higher incidence of light scattering within the photoanode.

The COMSOL software has been employed to investigate the effect of light scattering in various photoanode structures, as demonstrated by Lee et al. [160] (see Fig. 8.25A–D ). CdSe QDSSCs with TiO2 nanohelix photoanodes showed a 100% increase in the power conversion efficiency compared with conventional TiO2 nanoparticle photoanodes. The higher power conversion efficiency is attributed to the strong light-scattering properties of the TiO2 nanohelixes because of the unique geometry and near-single crystallinity of the TiO2 nanohelix structure.

Using the optimum metal oxide layer is important in DSSCs. One option to lower the recombination of photoexcited electrons is the insertion of a thin passivating/blocking layer of suitable metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO, SnO2, Nb2O5, etc.) in between the thick metal oxide layer and conducting substrate [159]. Zhang et al. [161] employed in situ preparation for different ordered photoanode designs such as ZnO NRs, TiO2 NT arrays, and ZnO/TiO2 core-shell nanostructures using templated sol-gel methods (Fig. 8.26 ).

Rao et al. [162] synthesized a hierarchical vertically aligned TiO2 NW array with a branched design using hydrothermal methods for use in the fabrication of a CdS/CdSe co-sensitized solar cell assembled with Pt as the CE (Fig. 8.27A ). Another 3D vertically aligned hierarchical structure was prepared by Feng et al. [163]. The structure consisted of a TiO2 nanowire and ZnO nanowire or nanosheet and exhibited high light harvesting as a result of its large surface area and high QD-sensitizer loading (Fig. 8.27B).

The development of a novel hetero-structured nanoarray photoanode and 3D metal oxide nanotree arrays provides a promising scheme to fabricate efficient energy conversion DSSCs, QDSSCs, and polymer solar cells [163,164]. Chetia et al. [165] replaced the branched NRs with 3D hollow mesoporous ZnO microspheres (ZnO HMSP) on vertically aligned 1D ZnO NWs on a photoanode (Fig. 8.28 ). Their higher surface area, more efficient light scattering, and facilitated electrolyte diffusion properties resulted in QDSSCs with improved performance.

Multidimensional TiO2 hierarchical structure with mesoporous nanoribbons consisting of oriented aligned TiO2 nanocrystal has been fabricated by Wu et al. [166] using thermal decomposition of Ti-contained gelatinlike precursors. The very high loading of CdS/CdSe QDs has been seen in this structure because of its high surface area and broad pore size (see Fig. 8.29 ).

Researchers have developed many different shapes of the materials for more efficient photoanodes, such as the “donut” shape [167], hexagon-shaped nanodisk [168], and cauliflowerlike hollow microsphere [169]. Li et al. [170] grew ZnO nanosheet arrays on woven titanium wires using a hydrothermal preparation method for use in flexible QDSSCs (Fig. 8.30 ).

Recently, hollow micro- or nanostructures have been used in DSSCs and in QDSSCs. Some of their advantages include excellent multireflection and efficient incident sunlight scattering, easy accessibility of the inner spaces to electrolytes through meso- or microchannels in shell, and fast regeneration of the reduced/oxidized species at the interface of the sensitizer–electrolyte and electrolyte–CE [171]. Jafarzadeh et al. [172] reviewed the application of hallow materials in DSSCs and QDSSCs. Liao et al. [173] fabricated mesoporous hollow TiO2 (MHS-TiO2) spheres as photoanode in DSSCs with high crystallinity using a mesoporous carbon (MC) hollow sphere template. In another attempt, Hu et al. [174] fabricated double-shelled copper chalcogenide nanocages via Kirkendall diffusion and etching as highly efficient CE materials in QDSSCs (see Fig. 8.31 ) using Cu2O nanocubes as a sacrificial precursor.

However, there is some research that reports using hybrid, hollow semiconductors as photoanodes [175–177]. Three-dimensional fluorinated tin oxide (FTO) coated with TiO2 as the photoanode, which exhibits field-driven drift behavior, was fabricated by Liu et al. [178] to increase DSSC performance (Fig. 8.32A ). To increase light harvesting for improved efficiency in DSSCs (Fig. 8.32B), Yun et al. [179] loaded Au@Ag core–shell nanoparticles on the surface of TiO2 hollow nanoparticles using sol–gel and chemical deposition.

Hard template and sacrificial template-based synthesis methods can be used for the fabrication of different types of hollow composites. Chen et al. [180] reviewed (1) the preparation of nanoparticles on the external surface of hollow ceria, (2) the anchorage of nanoparticles (NPs) onto the inner surface of ceria, and (3) the encapsulation of nanoparticles by ceria to form the so-called yolk-shell structure (Fig. 8.33 ). These methods could be used for the fabrication of hollow nanomaterials in solar cell application as well; however, there are many limitations that must be addressed for these preparation methods.
Hollow-structured materials have also been employed in CEs [181–183] and electrolytes [184] in solar cells.

Fig. 8.34 presents the fabrication steps for the preparation of hollow Co9S8 nanoneedles as a CE in QDSSCs; they are directly grown on fluorinated tin oxide (FTO) substrates using a simple sacrificial template method. The efficiency of the prepared QDSSC greatly improved as a result of the higher electrocatalytic activity of the prepared electrode.

Kung et al. [186] successfully fabricated hollow microflower arrays (HMFAs) of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) with several 2D hollow nanopetals on each microflower on a conducting glass using ZnO microflower arrays as the template (see Fig. 8.35 ). The prepared PEDOT-HMFA film is used as the catalytic material on the CE of DSSCs to increase its efficiency.
CNTs have been used as an effective material in photoanode because of their large specific surface area, high aspect ratio, chemical stability, and high electron mobility [187–189].

Multiwalled CNTs (MWCNTs) are another promising option for photoanodes in QDSSC. Wei et al. [190] fabricated MWCNTs containing polyaniline to increase the reactive group on its surface for higher QD absorption (Fig. 8.36 ).

Meanwhile, there are many reports of the use of hollow carbon-based materials as CEs [191,192]. Hierarchically structured hollow core–mesoporous shell carbon spheres have been used as CEs in CdSe QDSSCs [191].
Fig. 8.37 shows the preparation procedure used by Yang et al. [193] for N-doped hollow MC CEs.

Graphene has been gaining increasing attention in PV applications because of its ability to function in various SC components, such as in photoanodes, CEs, interfacial layers, sensitizers, etc. [194,195]. Its prominence is because of its high transmittance, compatibility, low sheet resistance, low cost, and flexibility. Further modification of graphene can occur to create different types of hybrids for use in semiconductor nanomaterials, MNMs, and CNTs [196,197]. Guo et al. [198] studied graphene-based materials, e.g., pristine graphene, GO, and reduced GO, as potential materials for photoanodes in DSSCs and their effects on the enhancement of the performance of the solar cells. The addition of small amounts of graphene sheets as additives to semiconducting materials could enhance the electron transfer process in the photoanode (see Figs. 8.38 and 8.39
). [198]. Meanwhile, graphene nanosheets could provide larger surface areas to anchor TiO2 nanoparticles compared with CNTs, offering a better option for capturing and transferring the photoinduced electrons in the photoanode. He et al. [199] employed graphene sheets and hetero-structured TiO2 nanocrystals using a solvothermal method to prepare QDSSCs.

The hybridization of graphene with SNMs has been studied for use in photoanodes in DSSCs [202]. The intermolecular forces, electrostatic binding, and charge transfer interactions among graphene and SNMs use graphene as a bridge for better extraction of photogenerated electrons from the sensitizer. Dutta et al. [203] used the hybrid graphene QDs-ZnO NWs in solar cells. Their ability to decrease the charge carrier recombination and improve light scattering resulted in developing increased efficiency. Tavakoli et al. [204] reported a good ECE (5.7%) for PbS-based solar cells employing ZnO/graphene QDs as CEs. Graphene–CNT hybrids are another promising option as an electrode material. Their large surface area and high electrical conductivity along with exceptional electron pathways could allow them to be competitive with Pt [205,206]. Some researchers [207] have fabricated graphene-MNMs hybrids to replace expensive conventional CEs such as Pt in solar cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The 2D Transition Metal Dichalcogenide Monolayers and Phosphorene Heterojunctions
ce:section.ce:para: Although 2D graphene exhibits promising optoelectronic properties for solar cell applications, its zero bandgap limitation has encouraged the investigation of different 2D materials, such as 2D TMDs (e.g., MoS2, WS2, TaS2, MoSe2, and WSe2, which have n-type characteristics) and single-layer black phosphorus (known as phosphorene, which has p-type characteristics). Transition metals (in groups IV, V, VI, and VII) and chalcogens (such as S, Se, and Te) have gained more attention because of their rich material compositions and suitable electronic properties [208–213]. Researchers have employed various methods for the preparation of TMD nanosheets, such as CVD growth, mechanical cleavage, wet chemical synthesis, and chemical Li-intercalation and exfoliation [214–216]. The application of solution-processable GO and 2D TMDs nanosheets in organic, dye-sensitized, QD and PSCs has been studied by Balis et al. [217]. Their tunable electronic structure via optimized functionalized routes demonstrates their suitability as possible hole–electron transport layers.

The 2D-TMD frameworks could allow better electron transfer layers for TiO2 scaffold-based photoanodes in QDSSCs and reduce charge carrier recombination [3]. Yang et al. [218] investigated the relationship among the nanostructure characteristics and solar cell performances in the design of advanced graphene-based materials. They provided general procedures for designing structures to achieve the required properties for specified applications.

Recently, aqueous sulfide–polysulfide redox electrolytes have gained attention as replacements for methanol-based polysulfide electrolytes in which methanol acts as a sacrificial hole scavenger and traditional polyiodide (I−/I3−) electrolytes in QDSSC [3,111,219,220]. Because using a liquid electrolyte increases the photoanode degradation potential, the use of novel quasi-solid electrolytes to increase the stability of QDSSC is very promising [111].

Meanwhile, research has been conducted to replace noble metal CEs with other promising materials such as CoS, CuS, Cu2S, CuS/CoS, MoS2, and PbS [221]. Other potential materials for use as CE are carbon nanomaterials, e.g., CNTs, porous carbon, and graphene [222–225]. Their popularity is derived from their good electrocatalytic activity, high thermal stability, high electrical conductivity, corrosion resistance, and low cost [3]. Different methods, such as SILAR [226], CBD [227], doctor-blading [228], spin-coating [225], drop-casting [229], sputtering [205], hydrothermal synthesis [230], vacuum deposition [231], electrochemical deposition [221], and successive ionic solution coating and reaction [232], have been employed for the preparation of CEs in QDSSCs. For the fabrication of flexible solar cell, some researchers have used chemically treated metal foils [170] or sheets [233] as CEs. For the preparation of CEs based on metal chalcogenides, SILAR and CBD are the preferred techniques; meanwhile, for the fabrication of metal thin-films or metal oxides, doctor-blading is desirable. The technical procedure of SILAR, CBD, and doctor-blading is similar to the procedure discussed for photoanode [111]. CNTs, MWCNTs, graphene, and their composites, which have large specific surface areas, high aspect ratio, chemical stability, and high electron mobility, could be applied as CE materials in QDDSCs because of their high potential to increase the conversion efficiency while remaining cost-effective. Lee et al. [234] presented the most popular conductive polymers and carbonaceous-type materials for the CEs of DSSCs (Fig. 8.40 ). Ti-mesh–supported MC CEs are an attractive choice because of their ability to decrease internal series resistance and downshift the redox potential of the polysulfide electrolyte [3]. Sun et al. [235] reviewed the use of 3D structures in photoanodes and CEs for increasing the performance of QDSSCs. The 3D structures exhibited the ability to increase QD and catalyst loading on their surfaces, to enlarge the solid–liquid interface, which lowers charge transfer resistance, to assist in charger transport and mass transfer, and to increase light harvesting, all of which led to the enhanced performance of the electrodes and QDSSCs.

For the preparation of more efficient DSSCs, Hsu et al. [236] fabricated a platinum-free CE composed of metal-organic-framework (MOF)–derived cobalt sulfide nanoparticles using the surfactant-assisted synthesis of an MOF (ZIF-67), which showed good potential for the preparation of low-cost Pt-free DSSCs.

The working concept is the same for DSSCs and QDSSCs. However, the dye sensitizer in DSSCs is replaced with QDs in QDSSCs. Many parameters influencing the efficiency of solar cells, such as photoanodes and CE design, sensitizer properties, and preparation methods, are the same for DSSCs and QDSSCs. As mentioned before, photoanodes greatly influence efficiency by collecting and transporting electrons from the dye sensitizer to the external circuit. Wide-bandgap semiconducting metal oxides such as TiO2, SnO2, ZnO, NbO5, and SrTiO3 are coated on the transparent conducting substrates and are commonly used photoanodes in DSSCs. The bandgap, morphology, composition, and thickness of semiconductor metal oxides affect the performance of the photoanode. The effect of morphology and the use of carbon-based materials in photoanodes have been discussed. Sengupta et al. [159] reviewed the effects of doping, morphology, and film thickness of photoanode materials for DSSCs. Lee et al. [234] reviewed the application of various organic materials in photoanodes, quasi/all-solid-state electrolytes, and metal element-free electrocatalytic films in DSSCs with cell efficiencies of >5%. The use of metal chalcogenides as perovskite sensitizers with ABX3 (ACH3NH3, BPb, Sn, and XCl, Br, I) structures could significantly increase the performance of solid-state DSSCs. However, more research is necessary to resolve certain disadvantages, such as their high affinity toward water, which leads to fast degradation [237].

Many disadvantages exist for the use of traditional liquid electrolytes in DSSCs, such as their poor thermal stability and corrosive nature toward the Pt on the surface of the CE. As a result, solid-state, quasi solid-state, and solvent-free DSSCs based on an ionic liquid electrolyte and using SeCN−/(SeCN)3 − as the redox couple have been developed to address these disadvantages [238]. Wu et al. [239] reviewed the various electrolytes used, their structure, and their application in DSSCs. One of the most promising solid-state electrolyte categories is solid hole-transporting materials (HTM). Different types of HTMs are available, such as 2,2′,7,7′-tetrakis(N,N-di-p-methoxyphenylamine)-9,9′spirobifluorene (Spiro-OMeTAD) [11], poly(3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl) (P3HT) [240], poly-triarylamine [241], and other HTMs [242,243], which could improve the performance of solar cell [244]. Su'ait et al. [245] reviewed the most recent electrolyte materials used in DSSCs. They claimed that a polyionic liquid polymer electrolyte (PolyIL) is an iodine-free electrolyte, which combines both the novel properties of ILs and improved mechanical durability and dimensional control resulting from polymerization, is the next-generation electrolyte for DSSCs. PolyILs could increase cell durability and photocurrent and reduce recombination and photocorrosion effects of the CE, which result from iodine radicals in the electrolyte. PolyILs are prepared via doping of polymers with ILs, in situ polymerization of vinyl monomers in ILs, and polymerization of polymerizable ILs (containing vinyl groups) [246]. The dyes that act as sensitizers on the metal oxide surfaces in DSSCs are classified into organometallics (containing transition metals) and organic dyes (containing organic chromophores). The stability, light absorption, electron transfer, and anchoring abilities with metal oxides could affect their efficiency.
Currently, ruthenium-based organometallic sensitizers, such as N3, N719, Z907, and black dye, are used in the most efficient DSSCs [247–249].

Meanwhile, according to recent studies, the use of nanotechnology in the preparation of specified electrolyte molecules and the use of hybrid perovskite dyes could present new challenges for improving the performance of DSSCs [159,250,251].

Schottky solar cells, which contain CQDs, are considered the simplest type of photovoltaic device. The advantages of this low-cost solar cell configuration include a facile solution-processed QD preparation, such as spray-coating, dip-coating, inkjet printing, etc., and the application of a thin layer (∼100nm) of QDs as the absorber. Fig. 8.41A shows the typical device structure of a Schottky cell [252] and Fig. 8.41B the transfer-stamped solution-processed pyramidal electrodes in CQD solar cells for increasing its efficiency [253].

In consideration of the charge-carrier transport, drift and diffusion play an important role in Schottky solar cells. To achieve higher efficiencies, carrier extraction must occur before recombination. Different absorbing nanomaterials are available, such as Si [254], CdTe [255], PbS [256], PbSe [257], ZnO [258], and lead chalcogenides (PbS, PbSe, etc.) [259,260]. The variations in the efficiencies in Schottky solar cell derive from the film preparation conditions and the quality, type, and shape of the nanocrystals [252,261–263].

Nanotechnology has introduced enormous opportunities in materials science and engineering for improving performance in different applications. The use of nanomaterials in solar cells, especially third-generation solar cells, greatly influences various solar device components. Although remarkable improvements in efficiency have been achieved with the use of novel nanomaterials, morphology engineering, etc., it is still very challenging to control these influential parameters to develop high-performance solar cells. Deep understanding of the properties and behavior of nanomaterials of different sizes and morphologies for use as electrodes, sensitizers, electrolytes, etc., is vital for future improvements. Meanwhile, the merits and drawbacks of different component materials and device designs across different types of solar cells must be considered. Overall, because nanomaterials demonstrate great improvement potential in solar cell applications, promising advancements and increased progress are expected in the very near future.
